title: UTI & Pyelonephritis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Primary Care, Internal Medicine, Infectious Disease
objectives: Differentiate uncomplicated cystitis from complicated UTI/pyelonephritis
Apply IDSA guidelines for antibiotic selection
Understand when fluoroquinolones should be avoided
Select appropriate treatment duration based on infection type

vignette.text:
A 28-year-old woman presents with 2 days of fever, chills, left flank pain, and dysuria. She reports frequency and urgency for the past 4 days. No vaginal discharge. Sexually active. No recent antibiotic use.

Past Medical History: None

Medications: Oral contraceptive

Allergies: NKDA

questions.stem: This patient has fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness.
questions.question: How are urinary tract infections classified?

options.text: CLASSIFICATION (IDSA 2025): UNCOMPLICATED CYSTITIS: Lower UTI in premenopausal, non-pregnant women; No structural/functional abnormalities; Symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, urgency; COMPLICATED UTI (cUTI): Extends beyond bladder (includes pyelonephritis); Systemic signs: Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, chills; OR UTI with: Obstruction, structural abnormality, instrumentation, male sex, pregnancy; PYELONEPHRITIS = Complicated UTI (renal parenchymal involvement); Note: Diabetes/immunocompromise alone does NOT automatically = complicated; This patient: Fever + flank pain + CVA tenderness = PYELONEPHRITIS (cUTI)
All UTIs in women are uncomplicated
Pyelonephritis is classified as uncomplicated
Diabetes automatically makes UTI complicated

questions.explanation: UTI Classification (IDSA 2010/2025): UNCOMPLICATED CYSTITIS: Lower UTI limited to bladder. Premenopausal, non-pregnant women. No structural or functional urinary tract abnormalities. Symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort. NO systemic signs (fever, flank pain). COMPLICATED UTI (cUTI): Extends beyond the bladder to kidneys. Systemic signs of illness: Fever, chills, rigors, Flank pain, CVA tenderness. OR UTI associated with: Urinary tract obstruction (stones, stricture, BPH), Structural abnormality, Indwelling catheter or recent instrumentation, Male sex, Pregnancy. IMPORTANT UPDATES (IDSA 2025): Pyelonephritis = Complicated UTI. Diabetes or immunocompromise ALONE does not automatically classify as complicated. This patient: Fever (102.4°F), flank pain, CVA tenderness = PYELONEPHRITIS, which is by definition a COMPLICATED UTI.

questions.question: What are the common pathogens causing UTIs?

options.text: Resistance patterns don't affect treatment choice
Gram-positive organisms are most common
Anaerobes are primary UTI pathogens

COMMON UTI PATHOGENS: E. coli: 75-90% of uncomplicated UTIs, 50-70% of complicated UTIs; Other Enterobacteriaceae: Klebsiella (10-15%), Proteus (especially in men/stones); Enterococcus: More common in healthcare-associated; S. saprophyticus: Second most common in young women (5-15% of uncomplicated cystitis); Pseudomonas: Healthcare-associated, catheter-related; RESISTANCE CONCERNS: Rising fluoroquinolone resistance (>20% in many areas); ESBL-producing organisms increasing; Local antibiogram should guide empiric therapy

questions.explanation: UTI Microbiology: MOST COMMON PATHOGENS: E. coli: Dominant pathogen. 75-90% of uncomplicated UTIs. 50-70% of complicated UTIs. OTHER ENTEROBACTERIACEAE: Klebsiella pneumoniae (10-15%). Proteus mirabilis (associated with struvite stones, more common in men). Enterobacter spp. GRAM-POSITIVES: S. saprophyticus: Second most common in young sexually active women (5-15%). Enterococcus: More common in healthcare-associated, catheter-related. PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA: Healthcare-associated UTIs. Catheter-associated infections. May require anti-pseudomonal coverage. RESISTANCE PATTERNS (Increasing Concern): Fluoroquinolone resistance: >20% in many communities (exceeds threshold for empiric use). ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae: Increasing, especially in recurrent UTIs, recent antibiotic use, healthcare exposure. TMP-SMX resistance: Variable, often >20% in many areas. CLINICAL IMPLICATION: Know your local antibiogram! Avoid fluoroquinolones if used in past 12 months.

questions.stem: A different patient presents with uncomplicated cystitis.
questions.question: What is the treatment for uncomplicated cystitis?

options.text: 7-14 days of antibiotics required
IV antibiotics required for cystitis
Fluoroquinolones are first-line for all cystitis

IDSA 2010 FIRST-LINE OPTIONS: NITROFURANTOIN monohydrate/macrocrystals 100mg BID × 5 days (avoid if CrCl <30); TMP-SMX DS BID × 3 days (if local resistance <20% and no recent use); FOSFOMYCIN 3g single dose (less effective than above, but convenient); SECOND-LINE (avoid if possible - collateral damage): FLUOROQUINOLONES: Ciprofloxacin 250mg BID × 3 days; Reserve for when first-line agents cannot be used; boxed warnings limit use; BETA-LACTAMS: Less effective, 3-7 days needed; Options if allergies preclude above

questions.explanation: Uncomplicated Cystitis Treatment (IDSA 2010): FIRST-LINE OPTIONS (Preferred): NITROFURANTOIN: 100mg BID × 5 days (monohydrate/macrocrystals formulation). Excellent for cystitis, concentrates in urine. Avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min (inadequate urine concentration). Avoid if pyelonephritis suspected (doesn't achieve tissue levels). TMP-SMX: DS (160/800mg) BID × 3 days. Use only if local resistance <20%. Avoid if used in past 3 months. FOSFOMYCIN: 3g single dose. Less effective than nitrofurantoin/TMP-SMX. Good option for convenience/compliance issues. SECOND-LINE (Avoid if possible): FLUOROQUINOLONES (Ciprofloxacin 250mg BID × 3 days): boxed warnings (tendon rupture, neuropathy, aortic aneurysm). Reserve for when first-line agents cannot be used. Contributes to resistance (collateral damage). BETA-LACTAMS (Amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalexin): Less effective than above options. Longer duration needed (5-7 days). Use if allergies preclude other options.

questions.stem: This patient may be manageable as an outpatient.
questions.question: What is the outpatient treatment for uncomplicated pyelonephritis?

options.text: All pyelonephritis requires hospitalization

OUTPATIENT PYELONEPHRITIS (IDSA 2010): FLUOROQUINOLONE: Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID × 7 days, OR Levofloxacin 750mg daily × 5 days; Use if local FQ resistance <10% and no recent FQ use; ALTERNATIVES: TMP-SMX DS BID × 14 days (if susceptible); Initial IV dose option: Ceftriaxone 1g IV × 1 dose then oral agent; ALWAYS obtain urine culture for pyelonephritis (tailor therapy); UPDATED (2025): 5-7 days FQ or 7 days non-FQ for cUTI; Avoid FQ if used in past 12 months
Nitrofurantoin is appropriate for pyelonephritis
Cultures are not needed for pyelonephritis

questions.explanation: Outpatient Pyelonephritis Treatment: WHO CAN BE TREATED OUTPATIENT: Mild-moderate illness, No vomiting (can take oral meds), Not septic, Reliable follow-up, Can return if worsening. IDSA 2010 RECOMMENDATIONS: FLUOROQUINOLONE (if local resistance <10% and no recent use): Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID × 7 days, OR Levofloxacin 750mg PO daily × 5 days. TMP-SMX DS BID × 14 days: Only if susceptibility confirmed (culture before treatment). Less effective empirically due to resistance. INITIAL IV DOSE OPTION: Ceftriaxone 1g IV × 1 dose in ED/clinic THEN Oral fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX to complete course. Improves outcomes if resistant organism. IDSA 2025 UPDATES FOR cUTI: 5-7 days of fluoroquinolone OR 7 days of non-fluoroquinolone. Avoid fluoroquinolones if exposure in past 12 months. CRITICAL: ALWAYS obtain urine culture for pyelonephritis! Tailor therapy based on susceptibilities. Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin NOT appropriate for pyelonephritis (no tissue penetration).

questions.stem: You decide this patient needs hospitalization.
questions.question: What is the inpatient treatment for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis?

options.text: Cannot transition to oral antibiotics
14 days of IV antibiotics required
Carbapenems are first-line for all inpatient UTI

INPATIENT cUTI/PYELONEPHRITIS (IDSA 2025): EMPIRIC OPTIONS (4-step selection process): WITHOUT sepsis: 3rd/4th gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily), Fluoroquinolone IV/PO, Piperacillin-tazobactam (avoid carbapenems initially); WITH sepsis: Same options PLUS carbapenems acceptable; STEP-DOWN TO ORAL: Once clinically improving, tolerating PO, and susceptibilities available; Options: Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, TMP-SMX (if susceptible); DURATION: 5-7 days total (fluoroquinolone) or 7 days (non-fluoroquinolone); Avoid fluoroquinolones if used in past 12 months

questions.explanation: Inpatient cUTI/Pyelonephritis (IDSA 2025): 4-STEP EMPIRIC SELECTION: Step 1: Sepsis status (determines urgency and breadth of coverage). Step 2: Recent antibiotic exposure (avoid if used in past 12 months). Step 3: Patient-specific factors (allergies, contraindications). Step 4: Local antibiogram (for sepsis - target >80-90% susceptibility). EMPIRIC IV OPTIONS: WITHOUT SEPSIS: Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily (preferred), Cefepime 2g IV q8h, Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h, Fluoroquinolone IV. Avoid carbapenems initially (stewardship). WITH SEPSIS: Above options PLUS carbapenems acceptable (meropenem, ertapenem). IV-TO-ORAL TRANSITION: Criteria: Clinically improving, Tolerating oral intake, Effective oral antibiotic available. Oral options: Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID, Levofloxacin 750mg daily, TMP-SMX DS BID (if susceptible). DURATION: Fluoroquinolone: 5-7 days total. Non-fluoroquinolone: 7 days total. With bacteremia: 7 days total (shorter than traditional!).

questions.stem: You're deciding whether to admit this patient.
questions.question: When should patients with pyelonephritis be hospitalized?

options.text: HOSPITALIZATION INDICATIONS: Sepsis or hemodynamic instability; Unable to tolerate oral intake (vomiting); Pregnancy; Suspected obstruction (hydronephrosis, stone); Uncertain diagnosis; Unreliable for follow-up; Failed outpatient therapy; Immunocompromised with severe presentation; Significant comorbidities; Concern for complicated infection (abscess); This patient: Tachycardia (104), low-normal BP, high fever - could go either way; If tolerating PO and reliable, outpatient trial with initial IV dose is reasonable
Young age excludes need for hospitalization
All pyelonephritis requires hospitalization
Fever alone requires hospitalization

questions.explanation: Hospitalization Criteria for Pyelonephritis: ABSOLUTE INDICATIONS: Sepsis (SIRS + evidence of organ dysfunction) or hemodynamic instability. Intractable vomiting (cannot take oral antibiotics). Pregnancy (risk to mother and fetus). Known or suspected urinary tract obstruction. Uncertain diagnosis (need further workup). RELATIVE INDICATIONS: High fever with significant symptoms. Failed outpatient therapy. Immunocompromised host. Significant comorbidities (poorly controlled DM, renal insufficiency). Unreliable follow-up. Social factors (homelessness, no support). Concern for complication (abscess). CAN LIKELY TREAT OUTPATIENT: Mild-moderate symptoms. Tolerating oral intake. Hemodynamically stable. No complicating factors. Reliable for follow-up. Can return if worsening. MIDDLE GROUND APPROACH: Initial IV antibiotic dose (ceftriaxone 1g) in ED. Observe for few hours. Discharge with oral antibiotics if tolerating and improving. Close follow-up in 24-48 hours.

questions.stem: You encounter patients with resistant organisms.
questions.question: How should ESBL-producing organisms be treated?

options.text: ESBL-PRODUCING ENTEROBACTERIACEAE (IDSA AMR Guidance): CYSTITIS: TMP-SMX (if susceptible), Nitrofurantoin (if susceptible, for cystitis only), Fosfomycin (efficacy data limited), Avoid cephalosporins (even if appears susceptible in vitro); PYELONEPHRITIS/cUTI: TMP-SMX or Fluoroquinolone (if susceptible) - preferred oral options; Carbapenems (ertapenem, meropenem) if resistant to above or critically ill; Step down from carbapenem to oral TMP-SMX/FQ if susceptible; Piperacillin-tazobactam: Conflicting data, avoid for ESBL bacteremia
Resistance testing doesn't guide therapy
Carbapenems required for all ESBL infections
Cephalosporins are adequate for ESBL UTI

questions.explanation: ESBL-Producing Organisms (IDSA AMR Guidance 2024): UNCOMPLICATED CYSTITIS: TMP-SMX: Preferred IF susceptible. Nitrofurantoin: Good option if susceptible, adequate for cystitis. Fosfomycin: Limited efficacy data, but can consider. AVOID: Cephalosporins (even if appears susceptible in vitro - may fail clinically). PYELONEPHRITIS/cUTI: PREFERRED (if susceptible): TMP-SMX, Ciprofloxacin or Levofloxacin. These achieve adequate urinary and tissue concentrations. CARBAPENEMS (ertapenem, meropenem, imipenem): Use if: TMP-SMX and fluoroquinolones resistant, Critically ill patient (can step down later), Failed other therapy. Step down to oral TMP-SMX/FQ when susceptibilities confirmed. PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM: Conflicting data for ESBL bacteremia (MERINO trial). Avoid for ESBL UTI with bacteremia. KEY PRINCIPLE: Avoid unnecessary carbapenem use (stewardship). Transition to narrower-spectrum oral agents when culture data available.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: 14 days IV antibiotics required

Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS: Pyelonephritis/cUTI - fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, pyuria, bacteriuria; (2) DISPOSITION DECISION: Borderline - tachycardia and fever suggest ill, but if tolerating PO and reliable → outpatient trial possible; If any concern → admit; (3) ALWAYS OBTAIN URINE CULTURE; (4) EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTIC: If outpatient: Ceftriaxone 1g IV × 1 dose in ED then Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID or Levofloxacin 750mg daily × 7 days total; If inpatient: Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily; (5) STEP DOWN to oral when improving, tolerating PO, cultures available; (6) DURATION: 5-7 days total; (7) FOLLOW-UP: 48-72 hours if outpatient
Nitrofurantoin for 5 days
No culture needed, treat empirically only

questions.explanation: Complete Management for this patient: STEP 1 - CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: Pyelonephritis/Complicated UTI: Fever 102.4°F, Left flank pain, CVA tenderness, Dysuria with pyuria and bacteriuria. STEP 2 - DISPOSITION: Borderline for outpatient management. Concerning: Tachycardia 104, fever 102.4°F. Reassuring: Young, healthy, likely can tolerate PO. Decision: If reliable and tolerating PO → outpatient with close follow-up. If any concern → admit. STEP 3 - OBTAIN CULTURES: Urine culture ALWAYS for pyelonephritis. Blood cultures if septic-appearing. Tailor therapy based on results. STEP 4 - EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTIC: OUTPATIENT approach: Ceftriaxone 1g IV × 1 dose in ED THEN Oral fluoroquinolone (if no recent FQ use): Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID or Levofloxacin 750mg daily. Alternative: TMP-SMX DS BID (if low resistance, susceptibility pending). INPATIENT approach: Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily (or cefepime, pip-tazo). STEP 5 - DURATION: 5-7 days total (per IDSA 2025). Adjust based on clinical response and culture. STEP 6 - FOLLOW-UP: Outpatient: Return in 48-72 hours, sooner if worsening. Review culture results and adjust therapy.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected uti & pyelonephritis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in uti & pyelonephritis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of uti & pyelonephritis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing uti & pyelonephritis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 75 cells/μL and presents with subacute headache, fever, and confusion. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the recommended initial treatment?

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole
options.explanation: Positive India ink indicates Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. Per IDSA/WHO guidelines, induction therapy is amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg/day × 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200mg/day. Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).

options.text: IV fluconazole 800mg/day as monotherapy
options.explanation: Fluconazole monotherapy for induction has significantly higher mortality than amphotericin-based regimens. It is used for consolidation and maintenance, not induction.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.explanation: The India ink positivity confirms cryptococcal meningitis. Bacterial meningitis coverage is not indicated when the organism is identified. Antifungal therapy is required.

options.text: Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B
options.explanation: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to UTI & Pyelonephritis including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for UTI & Pyelonephritis.
